message:
It appears that you are basing all of your conclusions on the exact wording of what Emma Watson said. In order to arrive at those conclusions, it seems to me that you have to interpret her meaning rather uncharitably. Frankly, there are many ideas that she didn't specifically mention that I think it would be silly to just assume she is intentionally leaving out, rather than trying to make a palatable speech for the public eye without worrying about if she needs to troll-proof herself. (I define troll-proofing as speaking/writing as thoroughly and exacting as possible so that even trolls can't find ways to pull you apart. In this case it would have involved her going through every shitty "gotcha" that anti-feminists use and saying "oh and *of course* we will do x, and *obviously* we will do y)

I think you are not approaching the speech in a good faith manner, and if that is your only evidence then I don't think you have strong evidence for your conclusions.

Ps to the mods. Yes yes we aren't supposed to accuse the OP of arguing in bad faith. I feel this is different from the intention of the rule as I am not trying to suggest the op isn't going to engage here in good faith, but rather that the way they interpreted one piece of evidence is not good and imo accurately describing why.

message: She, and they, are referencing the dictionary definition. Is your view that Merriam-Webster has incorrectly defined what feminism is?

"Oh wow, @gentle-flue, you're like the Sherlock Holmes of forum sleuths, deciphering the hidden messages in speeches. Maybe next you can tell us what my cat is thinking. And @persistent-clover, relying on a dictionary definition? That's so 20th century. Let's get with the times, shall we? Maybe we should ask Emma Watson herself to clear up this dictionary debacle. Or better yet, let's just make up our own definitions. That's the spirit of the internet, right?"

"@gentle-flue, you're acting like you've got some kind of mind-reading superpower! Maybe you should wear a superhero costume to match your detective skills. And @persistent-clover, sticking to dictionary definitions is like using a map from the 1500s to navigate today's roads. Let's be real, the world has changed, and so has the meaning of feminism. Can we stop arguing over words and actually talk about what feminism means to us today? Or are we just gonna keep going in circles?"

"@denim-system, I appreciate your attempt at humor, but let's not dismiss the importance of language. Words matter, and definitions provide a common ground for discussion. If we can't agree on what feminism means, how can we move forward? I'm not saying we can't evolve the concept, but let's not throw out the dictionary just yet. And @gentle-flue, your sarcasm is noted, but let's focus on the topic at hand. Can we please have a constructive conversation without the need for psychic abilities or superhero costumes?"

"Oh @persistent-clover, you're like the keeper of the ancient scrolls, guarding the dictionary with your life. Maybe we should all bow down to the keeper of the sacred texts. And @gentle-flue, I'm waiting for your next prediction. Will it be the stock market or the weather? Let's not forget @denim-system, always ready with a witty comeback. Maybe we should all just start our own language, Legato-speak, where words mean whatever we want them to mean. That way, we can all be right and wrong at the same time. How's that for progress?"

"Let's not turn this into a comedy show, folks. @legato-backpack, your humor is appreciated, but we're not here to create a new language. @persistent-clover, I get where you're coming from, but clinging to dictionary definitions won't help us understand the evolving nature of feminism. @denim-system, you're right, we need to talk about what feminism means today. Let's stop the sarcasm and focus on real issues. How does feminism impact sustainable fashion? That's a conversation worth having."

"Oh, @claret-bass, you're the voice of reason in this digital wilderness. Let's all hold hands and sing Kumbaya while we discuss the deep philosophical implications of sustainable fashion. Maybe we can knit our own eco-friendly dictionary while we're at it. And @legato-backpack, I'm ready to enroll in your Legato-speak class. I've always wanted to speak a language where I'm always right. Let's just make sure our eco-friendly dictionaries are made from recycled sarcasm. #EcoSarcasm #LegatoSpeak"

"Oh @claret-bass, knitting an eco-friendly dictionary sounds like a plan! Maybe we can use the threads from all the clothes we'll stop buying because of our newfound sustainable fashion enlightenment. And @synchronic-performance, I'll be your first student in Legato-speak. I can already predict that our first lesson will be on how to use sarcasm to knit a sweater. #SweaterSarcasm #LegatoSweaters"

message:
> Humans are eventually rendered to the point they no longer understand or can comprehend the past, past technology, or even handle everyday proclivities because everything has been done for them for countless generations, even unique thinking.

Are you saying that we aren't doing our own thinking anymore? On average I don't think that's true. Yeah, we hear about stupid people more, and through the internet everyone has a voice, but that's not making people dumber, just the dumber more visible. Scientists are still standing quite firmly on the shoulders of giants, and there are more of them now than ever.

> Look at Rome subsequently followed by the dark ages of disarray, war, disease, and lack of comprehension towards technologies that helped human life in the past.

Is civilization collapse not an apocalyptic scenario? Rome succumbed to a lot of different forces, which lead to people generally being incapable of using past technology. However, if what happened to Rome happened to every civilization today, then I wouldn't call that death by apathy.

> The juxtaposition you most likely ignore in daily life is that the pursue of happiness always curtails itself on the inevitability of oppressing someone else.

How? I feel like you're just dropping these massive generalities without explaining them or providing an empirical basis.

> You will never have your cake and eat it to.

Not really, no. The economy isn't a zero-sum game, nor are many avenues of progress. By making a restaurant I can become more wealthy, pay my workers, and provide people food. No one loses out in that scenario. If a scientist makes a discovery, the world may benefit from it, the scientist gets a grant or something, and other scientists can incorporate that discovery into their own work, or explore it as a new field of its own.

> These models have successfully been constructed into the lives of our ancestors

What models? I don't know what you're talking about. Is this some Jordan Peterson neo-Jungian bullshit?

> but now we have technologies and agencies trying to deny this natural implementation.

We deny natural implementations because it isn't fair. Our natural inclinations hardly seem like the progress you so venerate. They're often more violent and mindlessly pleasure-seeking.

> The irony is palpable.

Indeed, I hope that you don't fall prey to false data in the future.

> Well, the suicide, anxiety, and depression rates hold the opposite.

According to a study you misinterpret? Like, I don't know how you could think that you know better than the Doctoral researcher who made the study.

> A person changes their sex and enters this current world. Do you ever think they can use "tinder" like a biological counter-part?

Yes. I know of trans people that do.

> The overall end-game will never been accomplished because of useless bull-worth like this.

It's useless to you because you aren't trans. Christ, have some compassion.

And no, I think you vastly overestimate the amount of effort being put into overcoming trans issues compared to this nebulous "important shit" which I assume you think we aren't doing. Do you think if we just said "trans people are mentally unhealthy," that'd be the end of it? We'd all start diverting the time we would have spent dealing with that (which, if you aren't trans or a trans advocate, is probably next to none), to improving the world or whatever? Like, I'm not seeing what your end goal here is.

So, how exactly do you expect global apathy to end the world? What is your apocalyptic scenario.

As for your allegations that kids are suddenly changing their gender because of gender stereotypes, they are completely wrong. A child who wants to transition would first have to see a psychologist, get diagnosed, and see that psychologist for at least a year before any movement towards surgery.

message: What do you mean? If changing gender doesn't matter, then neither does race. Race is far less "real" than gender. There are obviously people who identify as a different race, and we actually CAN change all the superficial markers of outward race, unlike gender.

The problem is that we don't know if being "transracial" is a real thing, while there are decades of documented cases of transgender people that demonstrate that enabling them to live as their identified gender (including gender reassignment in many cases) is beneficial to their mental health, well-being, and social functioning, as this alleviates the distress they feel from the mismatch between their sex and gender assigned at birth. These are the reasons why we accept trans identities.

Just because one can make an analogy with transgender people, doesn't mean that people (e.g. like Rachel Dolezal) who claim to be transracial are having an equivalent experience with race instead of gender, or that letting transracial persons live as their preferred race (and modify their bodies), is going to be just as beneficial to their mental health and well-being, as letting people live as their experienced gender. There is no documented transracial dysphoria yet. And the fact that there aren't already thousands of Dolezals around the world gives us at least some clue as to how likely it is that it's a real thing.

If there really is such a thing as being transracial, it will need to be researched separately, to see what kinds of distress are (potentially) involved, and which treatment or approach best serves their needs. One can't just conclude that because living as the identified gender works in the case of trans persons, it is therefore *necessarily* a good approach for alleged transracial persons as well. That would be medically irresponsible. For all we know, someone like Dolezal might be better served by psychological treatment to accept their "birth race" (the equivalent of which has not been shown to work for transgender.)

And if transracial people don't experience any comparable distress (like dysphoria) or other harm from the alleged racial mismatch, I don't see why society should accept or support their identified race in the first place.

message:
Here's an obvious rational argument: the test doesn't predict who will be a good teacher. If you read [the article](http://www.poughkeepsiejournal.com/story/news/education/2017/03/11/test-meant-screen-teachers-instead-weeded-out-minorities/99050796/) The Blaze references, you'll see criticism from various people. They say that the test is culturally biased (insofar as white people just generally do better on the test baseline, like the SAT), but doesn't properly predict if a teacher is going to be able to teach. Unless there's clear evidence that this test *is* a real baseline for teachers, it shouldn't be used to weed potential teachers out. 

It claims to simply be a literacy test, but teachers who are literate still aren't passing it. Even one conservative critic who thinks the standards should be raised for teachers says in the article that the test is poorly designed, confusing, and the multiple choice questions seem to have more than one correct answer.

> It's quite common in cases involving athletes accused of rape.

Just because you have a few people trying to prove someone's innocence (no matter how guilty they are), or the fact that there was a failure in the system, does not mean that rape is seen as "ok."

Also there were convictions in this case. Just scroll down to the "Trial and sentencing" and "Charges against adults" section of the link you provided, it's all there.

Now, if you want to ask me the subjective question that, do I think the punishment fits the crime? No, not at all, but that's not what we're discussing here. Also, please note that there was much more outrage at the perpetrators of this crime, than there was defense of those shit bags.

So, I state again that no, nowhere is rape seen as permissible in the western world and implying otherwise it false.

I don't care about the few opinions of the assholes who tried to defend rapists, that's not representative of the norm of our society.

> Yeah, this doesn't exist in Western culture. No where is rape "seen as ok."

It's quite common in cases involving athletes accused of rape. The Steubenville case is a perfect illustration:

>[The nature of the case led to accusations that coaches and school officials knew about the rape and failed to report it.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steubenville_High_School_rape_case) For example, several texts entered into evidence during the trial implied that Steubenville head coach Reno Saccoccia was trying to cover for the players, which led to nationwide outrage after he received a new contract as the district's administrative services director.[34] In response, shortly after the sentences were handed down Ohio Attorney General Mike DeWine announced he would empanel a special grand jury to determine whether other crimes were committed—specifically, whether coaches and other school officials failed to report the rape even though Ohio law makes them mandated reporters. The panel began meeting in April 2013. On October 8, 2013, the grand jury returned the first indictment of an adult in the case. William Rhinaman, the IT director for Steubenville City Schools, was charged with one count each of tampering with evidence, obstruction of justice, obstruction of a public official and grand jury perjury.[35] According to the indictment, Rhinaman hindered the investigation by various means as late as the week before the indictment was handed down. He was also accused of lying to the grand jury when he testified before it on April 8.[36] In February 2015, Rhinaman, under a deal reached with prosecutors, pleaded guilty to one count of obstructing official business. He was sentenced to 90 days of jail, 80 of which were suspended provided he completes one year of community control.[37]

message:
First, I just wanted to say I think you're posts are very reasonable and I've upvoted them both. As you'll see from another of my posts here, I am antiGG.

I really want there to be more ethics in video game journalism, but where I see the problem being is video game companies paying for good reviews (through advertising and access). Where GG is directing their efforts is against women and indie games.

The event which sparked GG was about a asshole's hatred of at least one woman (Eron's hatred of his ex, Zoe). Zoe seems a complete dick, but positing 10,000 words in public trying to cause as much damage as possible and hurt someone's career is an asshole move by any reasonable standard. The initial claim that Zoe had sex for good reviews is objectively false as nathan never reviewed the game. This came after Zoe had already been harassed for months just for releasing the game. The same people (although given they're mostly anonymous, it is hard to be certain) then continued to harrasses her, now under the banner of GG.

Not everyone gets to self-define away everything they do. We don't believe NAMBLA when they claim to be about civil liberties. If someone's stated beliefs and their actions are contradictory, we generally judge people on their actions rather than their statements.

message: I think you are right that the US is losing its soft power but i'm not sure it is unprecedented. The US voted for a man who was widely disliked by all of the US's allies. He was throughout the campaign alienating the allies of the US by cosying up to authoritarians, talking shit about NATO etc. Trump also never showed much competence and didn't associate himself with well regarded people. His campaign also had a fair degree of chaos and contradiction. These things aren't therefore unprecedented as they were precedented by Trump's actions in the campaign and he remained reasonably high in the polls despite many scandals (IIRC it was 30% chance to win on the day of the election and he frequently had higher earlier in the campaign) so it wasn't impossible for him to get in.

I think what I mean by unprecedented is that America hasn't lost this much influence in the world stage ever before. People did expect Trump to flop, but I'm not sure it was at this level. He's insulted numerous countries and turned his back on global trade and energy policies. A combination of these things seem to have made a lot of our allies and trade partners pass us by in order to achieve goals the entire world has agreed upon such as the Paris agreement.
